2606.16244.title: SPARK: Security Knowledge Priming and Representation-Guided Knowledge Activation for LLM-based Secure Code Generation
2606.16244.paper_summary: Large language models routinely generate code with exploitable security flaws. Prior literature attributes this limitation to a lack of security expertise, steering current defense mechanisms toward heavy fine-tuning or external knowledge retrieval, which introduces significant computational overhead and data bias through redundant code examples. Contrary to this view, we argue that pretraining corpora are already rich in security material. The bottleneck is activation: without an explicit and brief cue, statistical pressure toward common training-distribution patterns suppresses the model's safety-relevant representations. We present SPARK, an inference-time security harness that activates this latent knowledge without any retraining. The harness has two parts. Component~I retrieves a few of the relevant Common Weakness Enumeration (CWE) entries for each coding task and appends a short structured cue to the prompt; this alone is enough to surface the model's existing security representations. Component~II adds a precomputed token bias to the logits at every decoding step. We obtain the bias by projecting a safe-direction vector, the unit difference between the mean safe and mean unsafe last-layer hidden states, through the language model head. The bias is computed once offline; applying it costs a single vector addition per generated token. We evaluate SPARK on 9 open-source models across C++, Java, and Python, and compare with 7 baselines spanning fine-tuning and retrieval-augmented methods. SPARK matches or improves on the best baseline in every setting while preserving HumanEval utility. We further test Component~I in a black-box setting on 7 of today's strongest models, including Claude, DeepSeek, and GPT, demonstrating the bottleneck of insecure code generation and the improvements enabled by our method.
2606.16244.paper_summary_zh: 大型語言模型經常生成具有可利用安全漏洞的代碼。先前的文獻將這一限制歸因於缺乏安全專業知識，導致目前的防禦機制傾向於進行重度微調或外部知識檢索，這會通過冗餘的代碼示例引入顯著的計算開銷和數據偏見。與此觀點相反，我們認為預訓練語料庫已經富含安全材料。瓶頸在於激活：如果沒有明確且簡短的提示，統計壓力會使模型的安全相關表示被壓制，趨向於常見的訓練分佈模式。我們提出了SPARK，一種推理時的安全工具，能夠在不進行任何再訓練的情況下激活這些潛在知識。該工具分為兩個部分。組件I為每個編碼任務檢索幾個相關的通用弱點枚舉（CWE）條目，並在提示中附加一個簡短的結構化提示；僅此一項就足以顯現模型現有的安全表示。組件II在每個解碼步驟中向logits添加一個預計算的標記偏置。我們通過將安全方向向量，即安全和不安全的最後一層隱藏狀態的均值之間的單位差異，投影到語言模型頭部來獲得偏置。偏置在離線時計算一次；應用它的成本是每生成一個標記進行一次向量加法。我們在C++、Java和Python的9個開源模型上評估SPARK，並與涵蓋微調和檢索增強方法的7個基準進行比較。在每個設置中，SPARK都能匹配或改進最佳基準，同時保持HumanEval的效用。我們還在當今最強的7個模型（包括Claude、DeepSeek和GPT）上以黑箱設置進一步測試組件I，展示了不安全代碼生成的瓶頸以及我們的方法所帶來的改進。
2606.16244.author: Xiaoyun Xu et.al.
2606.16244.authors: Xiaoyun Xu, Lichao Wu, Jona te Lintelo, Siyu Zhang, Stjepan Picek
